title: Setting Options (:set)
subtitle: How options work, and the conventions you'll see in every vimrc.
summary: :set toggles or assigns options. :set option turns it on; :set nooption turns it off; :set option! toggles; :set option=value assigns; :set option? queries.

blocks.text: Options are Vim's settings — there are over 300. Every vimrc is mostly `:set` calls. The syntax is consistent once you learn the conventions.

rows: Show options that differ from defaults
Show value AND where it was last set

blocks.title: Three Kinds of Options
blocks.text:
**Boolean** — on/off. Set with `:set name`, unset with `:set noname`, toggle with `:set name!`.

**Numeric** — integer value. Set with `:set name=N`.

**String** — text value. Set with `:set name=text`. Some are *flag lists* (`'formatoptions'`, `'complete'`) — use `+=` / `-=` / `^=` to add/remove/prepend without clobbering.

**Scope:** options are *global*, *buffer-local*, *window-local*, or *both* (with `setlocal` and `setglobal` versions). `:set` on a both-local option sets *both* the global default and the local copy. `:setlocal` sets only the local. `:verbose set X?` tells you where the current value was last assigned — invaluable for debugging mystery vimrc behavior.

**Short names** — most options have a 2-3 letter alias: `nu` = `number`, `ts` = `tabstop`, `sw` = `shiftwidth`, `et` = `expandtab`, `ic` = `ignorecase`. Vim docs use full names; vimrcs often use short.
When something behaves weirdly, run `:verbose set whatever?` — it tells you the script and line where the option was last set.
